g.data-narration: Page Replacement Algorithms - essential for understanding how operating systems manage memory efficiently.
g.text: Page Replacement Algorithms in Operating Systems

g.data-narration: When programs need more memory than available RAM, the OS must intelligently choose which pages to keep or discard.
g.text: Physical memory is limited but processes need more space. When RAM is full, the OS must
select victim pages to evict. This decision directly impacts system performance.

g.data-narration: Virtual memory extends physical RAM using disk space, creating the illusion of unlimited memory for processes.
g.text: Virtual memory combines RAM and disk to provide
more addressable space than physical memory allows.

g.data-narration: The page replacement process involves detecting faults, selecting victims, and updating page tables for memory management.
FIFO evicts the oldest page in memory using a simple queue. It's easy to implement but suffers from Belady's Anomaly.
g.text: Evicts the oldest page (first in queue). Simple queue data structure.
Suffers from Belady's Anomaly - more frames can cause MORE faults!

g.data-narration: LRU evicts the least recently used page, approximating optimal behavior. It avoids Belady's Anomaly but requires tracking usage history.
g.text: Evicts page not used for longest time in past. Better than FIFO, no Belady's Anomaly.
Implementation needs hardware counters or stack - significant overhead.

g.data-narration: OPT selects the page that won't be used for the longest time in the future. It produces minimum faults but requires impossible future knowledge.
g.text: Evicts page not needed for longest time in FUTURE. Theoretical benchmark.
Impossible to implement - needs oracle knowledge. Used to compare other algorithms.

g.data-narration: Clock algorithm uses a circular buffer with reference bits to approximate LRU with minimal overhead, commonly used in real systems.
g.text: Circular buffer with reference bit. Gives second chance if recently referenced.
Practical compromise - good performance with minimal overhead. Used in real OS!

g.data-narration: Belady's Anomaly shows counter-intuitive behavior: more memory frames can paradoxically increase page faults in FIFO.
g.text: Counter-intuitive phenomenon: more frames can cause MORE page faults!
Occurs in FIFO but NOT in stack algorithms (LRU). Example: ref string 1,2,3,4,1,2,3,4,5...

g.data-narration: Let's work through FIFO with 3 frames and reference string 7,0,1,2,0,3,0,4. Watch how pages get replaced.
Now let's see LRU with same reference string. Notice it replaces least recently used pages differently than FIFO.
Compare all algorithms: FIFO is simple but not optimal; LRU is better but harder to implement; Clock is the practical choice for real systems.
Remember: FIFO uses queue, LRU uses past usage, OPT is theoretical benchmark, Clock is practical choice. Practice numerical problems for exams.
